title: Solve Systems of Nonlinear Equations
text: Solve system nonlinear equations using Broyden Newton method choice global strategies line search trust region. options using numerical user supplied Jacobian, specifying banded numerical Jacobian allowing singular ill-conditioned Jacobian. Original Author: Berend Hasselman

title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — nleqslv-iterationreport
text: iteration reports consist series columns header summarising contents. Common column headers Iter Iteration counter Jac Jacobian type. Jacobian type indicated N Newton Jacobian B Broyden updated matrix; optionally followed letter s indicating totally singular matrix letter indicating ill-conditioned matrix. Unless Jacobian singular, Jacobian type followed estimate inverse condition number Jacobian parentheses computed Lapack. column blank backtracking active. Fnorm square euclidean norm function values / 2 Largest |f| infinity norm \(f(x)\) current point

title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — nleqslv-iterationreport
text: sample iteration report linesearch global methods (cline, qline gline) (intercolumn space removed make table fit) column headed Lambda shows value line search parameter. column headed Ftarg follows sufficient decrease requirement value Fnorm must drop current step accepted. value Lambda may lower threshold determined setting control parameter xtol avoid reporting false convergence. acceptable Lambda possible Broyden method used, new Jacobian computed.

title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — nleqslv-iterationreport
text: iteration report global method none almost report, except column labelled Ftarg omitted. column Lambda gives ratio maximum stepsize length full Newton step. either exactly 1.0, indicating Newton step smaller maximum stepsize therefore used unmodified, smaller 1.0, indicating Newton step larger maximum stepsize therefore truncated.

what: Report for the double dogleg global method
title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — nleqslv-iterationreport
text: sample iteration report global method dbldog (intercolumn space removed make table fit) column Jacobian letters indicate following C fraction (\(\le1.0\)) Cauchy steepest descent step taken fraction ratio trust region radius Cauchy steplength. W convex combination Cauchy eta*(Newton step) taken. number column headed Lambda weight partial Newton step. P fraction (\(\ge1.0\)) partial Newton step, equal eta*(Newton step), taken fraction ratio trust region radius partial Newton steplength. N normal full Newton step taken. number column headed Eta calculated upper limit ratio length steepest descent direction length Newton step. See Dennis Schnabel (1996) pp.139ff details. column headed Dlt0 gives trust region size start current iteration. column headed Dltn gives trust region size current step accepted dogleg algorithm. trust region size decreased actual reduction function value norm agree well predicted reduction linear approximation function exhibit sufficient decrease. increased actual predicted reduction sufficiently close. trust region size allowed decrease beyond threshold determined setting control parameter xtol; happens backtracking regarded failure terminated. case new Jacobian calculated Broyden method used. current trust region step continued doubled trust region size actual predicted reduction agree extremely well. indicated * immediately following value column headed Dltn. save gradient calculations. However, trial step never taken current step Newton step. trial step succeed previous trust region size restored halving trial size. exception trial step takes Newton step. case trust region size immediately set size Newton step implies halving new size leads smaller size trust region . Normally initial trust region radius final trust region radius previous iteration trust region size restricted size current Newton step. full Newton steps taken, trust region radius start iteration may less final value previous iteration. double dogleg method trust region updating procedure fully explained sections 6.4.2 6.4.3 Dennis Schnabel (1996).

what: Report for the single (Powell) dogleg global method
title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — nleqslv-iterationreport
text: sample iteration report global method pwldog (intercolumn space removed make table fit) much simpler double dogleg report, since single dogleg takes either steepest descent step, convex combination steepest descent Newton steps full Newton step. number column Lambda weight Newton step. single dogleg method special case double dogleg method eta equal 1. uses method updating trust region size double dogleg method.

what: Report for the hook step global method
title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — nleqslv-iterationreport
text: sample iteration report global method hook (intercolumn space removed make table fit) column headed mu shows Levenberg-Marquardt parameter Newton step larger trust region radius. column headed dnorm gives Euclidean norm step (adjustment current x) taken algorithm. absolute value difference Dlt0 less 0.1 times trust region radius. column Jacobian letters indicate following H Levenberg-Marquardt restricted step taken. N normal full Newton step taken. meaning columns headed Dlt0 Dltn identical columns double dogleg method. method updating trust region size double dogleg method.

what: The nleqslv package provides two algorithms for solving (dense) nonlinear systems of equations. The methods provided are  a Broyden Secant method where the matrix of derivatives is updated after each major iteration using the Broyden rank 1 update. a full Newton method where the Jacobian matrix of derivatives is recalculated at each iteration  Both methods utilize global strategies such as line search or trust region methods whenever the standard Newton/Broyden step does not lead to a point closer to a root of the equation system. Both methods can also be used without a norm reducing global strategy. Line search may be either cubic, quadratic or geometric. The trust region methods are either the double dogleg method, the Powell single dogleg method or a Levenberg-Marquardt type method. — nleqslv-package
title: The nleqslv package provides two algorithms for solving (dense) nonlinear systems of equations. The methods provided are  a Broyden Secant method where the matrix of derivatives is updated after each major iteration using the Broyden rank 1 update. a full Newton method where the Jacobian matrix of derivatives is recalculated at each iteration  Both methods utilize global strategies such as line search or trust region methods whenever the standard Newton/Broyden step does not lead to a point closer to a root of the equation system. Both methods can also be used without a norm reducing global strategy. Line search may be either cubic, quadratic or geometric. The trust region methods are either the double dogleg method, the Powell single dogleg method or a Levenberg-Marquardt type method. — nleqslv-package
text: facility specifying Jacobian banded; can significantly speedup calculation numerical Jacobian number sub- super diagonals small compared size system equations. example Jacobian tridiagonal system can calculated three evaluations function.

The methods provided are  a Broyden Secant method where the matrix of derivatives is updated after each major iteration using the Broyden rank 1 update. a full Newton method where the Jacobian matrix of derivatives is recalculated at each iteration  Both methods utilize global strategies such as line search or trust region methods whenever the standard Newton/Broyden step does not lead to a point closer to a root of the equation system. Both methods can also be used without a norm reducing global strategy. Line search may be either cubic, quadratic or geometric. Both methods can also be used without a norm reducing global strategy. Line search may be either cubic, quadratic or geometric. code: nleqslv(   x,   fn,   jac = NULL,   ...,   method = c("Broyden", "Newton"),   global = c("dbldog", "pwldog", "cline", "qline", "gline", "hook", "none"),   xscalm = c("fixed", "auto"),   jacobian = FALSE,   control = list() )

Function values may near zero; therefore user must check function values acceptably small. 3 better point found.  means algorithm stalled find acceptable new point.  may may indicate acceptably small function values. 4 Iteration limit maxit exceeded. 5 Jacobian ill-conditioned. 6 Jacobian singular. 7 Jacobian unusable. -10 User supplied Jacobian likely incorrect.  message string describing termination code scalex vector containing scaling factors, final values automatic scaling selected njcnt number Jacobian evaluations nfcnt number function evaluations, excluding required calculating Jacobian excluding initial function evaluation (iteration 0) iter number outer iterations used algorithm. excludes initial iteration.  number backtracks can calculated difference nfcnt iter components. jac final Jacobian Broyden approximation jacobian set TRUE.  iterations executed, may happen initial guess sufficiently close solution, Broyden approximation returned matrix always actual Jacobian.  matrix singular ill-conditioned returned matrix value.

text: Method Broyden starts computed Jacobian function updates Jacobian successful iteration using -called Broyden update. method often shows super linear convergence towards solution. nleqslv determines continue current Broyden matrix compute new Jacobian. Method Newton calculates Jacobian function fn iteration. Close solution method usually show quadratic convergence. methods apply -called (backtracking) global strategy find better (acceptable) iterate.  function criterion used algorithm half sum squares function values “acceptable” means sufficient decrease current function criterion value compared previous iteration. comprehensive discussion issues can found Dennis Schnabel (1996). methods apply unpivoted QR-decomposition Jacobian implemented Lapack. Broyden method applies rank-1 update Jacobian end iteration based simplified modernized version algorithm described Reichel Gragg (1990).

text: applying full Newton Broyden step yield sufficiently smaller function criterion value nleqslv attempt decrease steplength using one several -called global strategies. global argument indicates global strategy use use global strategy cline cubic line search qline quadratic line search gline geometric line search dbldog trust region method using double dogleg method described Dennis Schnabel (1996) pwldog trust region method using Powell dogleg method developed Powell (1970). hook trust region method described Dennis Schnabel (1996) locally constrained optimal (“hook”) step.  equivalent Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm described MoréMore (1978) Nocedal Wright (2006). none pure local Newton Broyden iteration used.  maximum stepsize (see ) taken account.  default maximum number iterations (see ) set 20. double dogleg method default global strategy employed package. global strategy use particular situation matter trial error. one trust region methods fails, one line search strategies tried. Sometimes trust region work sometimes line search method; neither clear advantage many cases double dogleg method works quite well. function solved returns non-finite function values parameter vector x algorithm evaluating numerical Jacobian, non-finite values replaced large number forcing algorithm backtrack, .e. decrease line search factor decrease trust region radius.

text: control argument named list can supply following components: xtol relative steplength tolerance.  relative steplength scaled x values smaller value convergence declared. default value \(10^{-8}\). ftol function value tolerance.  Convergence declared largest absolute function value smaller ftol.  default value \(10^{-8}\). btol backtracking tolerance.  relative steplength backtracking step find acceptable point smaller backtracking tolerance, backtracking terminated.  Broyden method new Jacobian calculated Jacobian outdated.  default value \(10^{-3}\). cndtol tolerance test ill conditioning Jacobian Broyden approximation. less machine precision silently set machine precision.  estimated inverse condition (approximated) Jacobian matrix less equal value cndtol matrix deemed ill-conditioned, case error reported allowSingular component set FALSE.  default value \(10^{-12}\). sigma Reduction factor geometric line search. default value 0.5. scalex vector scaling values parameters.  inverse scale value indication size parameter.  default value 1.0 scale values. maxit maximum number major iterations.  default value 150 global strategy specified.  global strategy specified default 20. trace Non-negative integer. value 1 give detailed report progress iteration. description see Iteration-report. chkjac logical value indicating whether check user supplied Jacobian, supplied. default value FALSE. first 10 errors printed.  code check derived code Bouaricha Schnabel (1997). delta Initial (scaled) trust region radius.  value \(-1.0\) "cauchy" replaced length Cauchy step initial point.  value \(-2.0\) "newton" replaced length Newton step initial point.  numeric value less equal 0 equal \(-2.0\), replaced \(-1.0\); algorithm start length Cauchy step initial point.  numeric positive set smaller value supplied maximum stepsize.  numeric one permitted character strings error message issued.  default \(-2.0\). stepmax Maximum scaled stepsize.  negative maximum stepsize set largest positive representable number. default \(-1.0\), default maximum stepsize. dsub Number non zero subdiagonals banded Jacobian. default assume Jacobian banded.  Must specified dsuper specified must larger zero dsuper zero. dsuper Number non zero super diagonals banded Jacobian.  default assume Jacobian banded. Must specified dsub specified must larger zero dsub zero. allowSingular logical value indicating small correction Jacobian singular ill-conditioned allowed.  correction less 100*.Machine$double.eps correction applied unusable Jacobian reported.  method used similar Levenberg-Marquardt correction explained Dennis Schnabel (1996) page 151.  may necessary choose higher value cndtol enforce correction.  default value FALSE.

(1997), Algorithm 768: TENSOLVE: Software Package Solving Systems Nonlinear Equations Nonlinear Least-squares Problems Using Tensor Methods, Transactions Mathematical Software, 23, 2, pp. 174–195. Dennis, J.E. Jr Schnabel, R.B. (1996), Numerical Methods Unconstrained Optimization Nonlinear Equations, Siam. MoréMore, J.J. (1978), Levenberg-Marquardt Algorithm, Implementation Theory, Numerical Analysis, G.. Watson (Ed.), Lecture Notes Mathematics 630, Springer-Verlag, pp. 105–116. Golub, G.H C.F. Van Loan (1996), Matrix Computations (3rd edition), John Hopkins University Press. Higham, N.J. (2002), Accuracy Stability Numerical Algorithms, 2nd ed., SIAM, pp. 10–11. Nocedal, J. Wright, S.J. (2006), Numerical Optimization, Springer. Powell, M.J.D. (1970), hybrid method nonlinear algebraic equations, Numerical Methods Nonlinear Algebraic Equations, P. Rabinowitz (Ed.), Gordon & Breach. Powell, M.J.D. (1970), Fortran subroutine solving systems nonlinear equations, Numerical Methods Nonlinear Algebraic Equations, P. Rabinowitz (Ed.), Gordon & Breach. decompositions, Reichel, L. W.B. Gragg (1990), Algorithm 686: FORTRAN subroutines updating QR decomposition, ACM Trans. Math. Softw., 16, 4, pp. 369–377.

title: Solving systems of nonlinear equations with Broyden or Newton — nleqslv
code: # Dennis Schnabel example 6.5.1 page 149 dslnex <- function(x) {     y <- numeric(2)     y[1] <- x[1]^2 + x[2]^2 - 2     y[2] <- exp(x[1]-1) + x[2]^3 - 2     y }  jacdsln <- function(x) {     n <- length(x)     Df <- matrix(numeric(n*n),n,n)     Df[1,1] <- 2*x[1]     Df[1,2] <- 2*x[2]     Df[2,1] <- exp(x[1]-1)     Df[2,2] <- 3*x[2]^2      Df }  BADjacdsln <- function(x) {     n <- length(x)     Df <- matrix(numeric(n*n),n,n)     Df[1,1] <- 4*x[1]     Df[1,2] <- 2*x[2]     Df[2,1] <- exp(x[1]-1)     Df[2,2] <- 5*x[2]^2      Df }  xstart <- c(2,0.5) fstart <- dslnex(xstart) xstart #> [1] 2.0 0.5 fstart #> [1] 2.2500000 0.8432818  # a solution is c(1,1)  nleqslv(xstart, dslnex, control=list(btol=.01)) #> $x #> [1] 1 1 #>  #> $fvec #> [1] 1.499777e-09 2.056389e-09 #>  #> $termcd #> [1] 1 #>  #> $message #> [1] "Function criterion near zero" #>  #> $scalex #> [1] 1 1 #>  #> $nfcnt #> [1] 12 #>  #> $njcnt #> [1] 1 #>  #> $iter #> [1] 10 #>   # Cauchy start nleqslv(xstart, dslnex, control=list(trace=1,btol=.01,delta="cauchy")) #>   Algorithm parameters #>   -------------------- #>   Method: Broyden  Global strategy: double dogleg (initial trust region = -1) #>   Maximum stepsize = 1.79769e+308 #>   Scaling: fixed #>   ftol = 1e-08 xtol = 1e-08 btol = 0.01 cndtol = 1e-12 #>  #>   Iteration report #>   ---------------- #>   Iter         Jac     Lambda      Eta     Dlt0     Dltn         Fnorm   Largest |f| #>      0                                                    2.886812e+00  2.250000e+00 #>      1  N(9.6e-03) C            0.9544   0.4671   0.9343* 1.699715e-01  5.421673e-01 #>      1             W   0.0833   0.9544   0.9343   0.4671  1.699715e-01  5.421673e-01 #>      2  B(1.1e-02) W   0.1154   0.4851   0.4671   0.4671  1.277667e-01  5.043571e-01 #>      3  B(7.3e-02) W   0.7879   0.7289   0.4671   0.0759  5.067893e-01  7.973542e-01 #>      3             C            0.7289   0.0759   0.1519  5.440250e-02  2.726084e-01 #>      4  B(8.3e-02) W   0.5307   0.3271   0.1519   0.3037  3.576547e-02  2.657553e-01 #>      5  B(1.8e-01) N            0.6674   0.2191   0.4383  6.566182e-03  8.555110e-02 #>      6  B(1.8e-01) N            0.9801   0.0376   0.0752  4.921645e-04  3.094104e-02 #>      7  B(1.9e-01) N            0.7981   0.0157   0.0313  4.960629e-06  2.826064e-03 #>      8  B(1.6e-01) N            0.3942   0.0029   0.0058  1.545503e-08  1.757498e-04 #>      9  B(1.5e-01) N            0.6536   0.0001   0.0003  2.968676e-11  5.983765e-06 #>     10  B(1.5e-01) N            0.4730   0.0000   0.0000  4.741792e-14  2.198380e-07 #>     11  B(1.5e-01) N            0.9787   0.0000   0.0000  6.451792e-19  8.118586e-10 #> $x #> [1] 1 1 #>  #> $fvec #> [1]  8.118586e-10 -7.945087e-10 #>  #> $termcd #> [1] 1 #>  #> $message #> [1] "Function criterion near zero" #>  #> $scalex #> [1] 1 1 #>  #> $nfcnt #> [1] 13 #>  #> $njcnt #> [1] 1 #>  #> $iter #> [1] 11 #>   # Newton start nleqslv(xstart, dslnex, control=list(trace=1,btol=.01,delta="newton")) #>   Algorithm parameters #>   -------------------- #>   Method: Broyden  Global strategy: double dogleg (initial trust region = -2) #>   Maximum stepsize = 1.79769e+308 #>   Scaling: fixed #>   ftol = 1e-08 xtol = 1e-08 btol = 0.01 cndtol = 1e-12 #>  #>   Iteration report #>   ---------------- #>   Iter         Jac     Lambda      Eta     Dlt0     Dltn         Fnorm   Largest |f| #>      0                                                    2.886812e+00  2.250000e+00 #>      1  N(9.6e-03) N            0.9544  10.1874   1.0187  5.787362e+05  1.070841e+03 #>      1             W   0.0932   0.9544   1.0187   1.0187  1.862204e+00  1.387696e+00 #>      2  B(1.9e-01) N            0.9658   0.6119   1.2239  1.003362e-01  4.468549e-01 #>      3  B(2.1e-01) N            0.6470   0.2521   0.5042  1.763418e-02  1.819978e-01 #>      4  B(1.3e-01) N            0.3486   0.2617   0.0598  5.952083e-02  3.020657e-01 #>      4             W   0.6248   0.3486   0.0598   0.1196  6.625459e-03  8.319100e-02 #>      5  B(1.5e-01) N            0.4212   0.1186   0.1186  3.368227e-03  5.868436e-02 #>      6  B(1.4e-01) N            0.9955   0.0177   0.0354  9.352918e-05  1.350724e-02 #>      7  B(1.8e-01) N            0.7560   0.0073   0.0146  9.330438e-06  3.933436e-03 #>      8  B(1.5e-01) N            0.8799   0.0020   0.0041  1.458619e-09  3.825434e-05 #>      9  B(1.5e-01) N            0.9969   0.0000   0.0000  1.640555e-13  4.501888e-07 #>     10  B(1.5e-01) N            0.9979   0.0000   0.0000  3.239033e-18  2.056389e-09 #> $x #> [1] 1 1 #>  #> $fvec #> [1] 1.499777e-09 2.056389e-09 #>  #> $termcd #> [1] 1 #>  #> $message #> [1] "Function criterion near zero" #>  #> $scalex #> [1] 1 1 #>  #> $nfcnt #> [1] 12 #>  #> $njcnt #> [1] 1 #>  #> $iter #> [1] 10 #>   # final Broyden approximation of Jacobian (quite good) z <- nleqslv(xstart, dslnex, jacobian=TRUE,control=list(btol=.01)) z$x #> [1] 1 1 z$jac #>           [,1]     [,2] #> [1,] 1.9818090 2.005414 #> [2,] 0.9750673 3.007420 jacdsln(z$x) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    2    2 #> [2,]    1    3  # different initial start; not a very good final approximation xstart <- c(0.5,2) z <- nleqslv(xstart, dslnex, jacobian=TRUE,control=list(btol=.01)) z$x #> [1] 1 1 z$jac #>          [,1]     [,2] #> [1,] 2.197044 2.182400 #> [2,] 3.210320 5.046055 jacdsln(z$x) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    2    2 #> [2,]    1    3  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # no global strategy but limit stepsize # but look carefully: a different solution is found nleqslv(xstart, dslnex, method="Newton", global="none", control=list(trace=1,stepmax=5))  # but if the stepsize is limited even more the c(1,1) solution is found nleqslv(xstart, dslnex, method="Newton", global="none", control=list(trace=1,stepmax=2))  # Broyden also finds the c(1,1) solution when the stepsize is limited nleqslv(xstart, dslnex, jacdsln, method="Broyden", global="none", control=list(trace=1,stepmax=2)) } # }  # example with a singular jacobian in the initial guess f <- function(x) {     y <- numeric(3)     y[1] <- x[1] + x[2] - x[1]*x[2] - 2     y[2] <- x[1] + x[3] - x[1]*x[3] - 3     y[3] <- x[2] + x[3] - 4     return(y) }  Jac <- function(x) {     J <- matrix(0,nrow=3,ncol=3)     J[,1] <- c(1-x[2],1-x[3],0)     J[,2] <- c(1-x[1],0,1)     J[,3] <- c(0,1-x[1],1)     J }  # exact solution xsol <- c(-.5, 5/3 , 7/3) xsol #> [1] -0.500000  1.666667  2.333333  xstart <- c(1,2,3) J <- Jac(xstart) J #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]   -1    0    0 #> [2,]   -2    0    0 #> [3,]    0    1    1 rcond(J) #> [1] 0  z <- nleqslv(xstart,f,Jac, method="Newton",control=list(trace=1,allowSingular=TRUE)) #>   Algorithm parameters #>   -------------------- #>   Method: Newton  Global strategy: double dogleg (initial trust region = -2) #>   Maximum stepsize = 1.79769e+308 #>   Scaling: fixed #>   ftol = 1e-08 xtol = 1e-08 btol = 0.001 cndtol = 1e-12 #>  #>   Iteration report #>   ---------------- #>   Iter         Jac     Lambda      Eta     Dlt0     Dltn         Fnorm   Largest |f| #>      0                                                    3.000000e+00  2.000000e+00 #>      1  N(0.0e+00) N            0.9535   1.2247   2.4495  2.500000e-01  5.000000e-01 #>      2  N(2.0e-01) N            0.8000   0.6124   1.2247  1.562500e-02  1.250000e-01 #>      3  N(2.6e-01) N            0.9660   0.1179   0.2357  2.999644e-28  1.731948e-14 all.equal(z$x,xsol) #> [1] TRUE

code: dslnex <- function(x) {     y <- numeric(2)     y[1] <- x[1]^2 + x[2]^2 - 2     y[2] <- exp(x[1]-1) + x[2]^3 - 2     y } xstart <- c(1.5,0.5) fstart <- dslnex(xstart) z <- testnslv(xstart,dslnex) print(z) #> Call: #> testnslv(x = xstart, fn = dslnex) #>  #> Results: #>     Method Global termcd Fcnt Jcnt Iter Message     Fnorm #> 1   Newton  cline      1    8    6    6   Fcrit 1.693e-28 #> 2   Newton  qline      1    8    6    6   Fcrit 1.693e-28 #> 3   Newton  gline      1   12    6    6   Fcrit 6.364e-20 #> 4   Newton pwldog      1    8    6    6   Fcrit 2.629e-23 #> 5   Newton dbldog      1    8    6    6   Fcrit 1.710e-26 #> 6   Newton   hook      1    8    6    6   Fcrit 3.753e-24 #> 7   Newton   none      1    7    7    7   Fcrit 8.470e-21 #> 8  Broyden  cline      1   12    1   10   Fcrit 5.315e-19 #> 9  Broyden  qline      1   12    1   10   Fcrit 5.315e-19 #> 10 Broyden  gline      1   19    1   10   Fcrit 3.919e-17 #> 11 Broyden pwldog      1   13    1   11   Fcrit 4.620e-21 #> 12 Broyden dbldog      1   13    1   11   Fcrit 1.041e-21 #> 13 Broyden   hook      1   13    1   10   Fcrit 1.548e-17 #> 14 Broyden   none      1   13    1   13   Fcrit 8.286e-18

text: testnslv returns object class "test.nleqslv" list containing following elements call matched call dataframe containing results following columns Method method used. Global global strategy used. termcd termination code nleqslv. Fcnt number function evaluations used method global strategy.  excludes function evaluations made computing numerical Jacobian. Jcnt number Jacobian evaluations. Iter number outer iterations used algorithm. Message string describing termination code abbreviated form. Fnorm square euclidean norm vector function results divided 2. cpusecs CPU seconds used requested number repetitions (present argument Nrep 0).  title description specified abbreviated strings Fcrit Convergence function values achieved. Xcrit means relative distance two consecutive x-values smaller xtol. Stalled algorithm find acceptable new point. Maxiter Iteration limit maxit exceeded. Illcond Jacobian ill-conditioned. Singular Jacobian singular. BadJac Jacobian unusable. ERROR nleqslv stopped fatal error.

code: dslnex <- function(x) {     y <- numeric(2)     y[1] <- x[1]^2 + x[2]^2 - 2     y[2] <- exp(x[1]-1) + x[2]^3 - 2     y } xstart <- c(0.5,0.5) fstart <- dslnex(xstart) testnslv(xstart,dslnex) #> Call: #> testnslv(x = xstart, fn = dslnex) #>  #> Results: #>     Method Global termcd Fcnt Jcnt Iter Message     Fnorm #> 1   Newton  cline      1    7    6    6   Fcrit 8.550e-23 #> 2   Newton  qline      1    7    6    6   Fcrit 8.550e-23 #> 3   Newton  gline      1    9    5    5   Fcrit 1.501e-20 #> 4   Newton pwldog      1    7    6    6   Fcrit 4.536e-27 #> 5   Newton dbldog      1    7    6    6   Fcrit 4.536e-27 #> 6   Newton   hook      1    7    6    6   Fcrit 2.450e-23 #> 7   Newton   none      1    8    8    8   Fcrit 6.085e-25 #> 8  Broyden  cline      1   12    1    9   Fcrit 4.297e-17 #> 9  Broyden  qline      1   12    1    9   Fcrit 4.297e-17 #> 10 Broyden  gline      1   14    1   10   Fcrit 4.960e-21 #> 11 Broyden pwldog      1   12    1   10   Fcrit 5.156e-21 #> 12 Broyden dbldog      1   12    1   10   Fcrit 3.958e-20 #> 13 Broyden   hook      1   12    1   10   Fcrit 1.127e-19 #> 14 Broyden   none      1   13    1   13   Fcrit 1.783e-22 # this will encounter an error xstart <- c(2.0,0.5) fstart <- dslnex(xstart) testnslv(xstart,dslnex) #> Error (method=Newton global=none): non-finite value(s) detected in jacobian (row=2,col=1) #> Call: #> testnslv(x = xstart, fn = dslnex) #>  #> Results: #>     Method Global termcd Fcnt Jcnt Iter Message     Fnorm #> 1   Newton  cline      1   11    7    7   Fcrit 4.992e-19 #> 2   Newton  qline      1   10    7    7   Fcrit 2.809e-20 #> 3   Newton  gline      1   17    7    7   Fcrit 6.311e-29 #> 4   Newton pwldog      1    6    5    5   Fcrit 1.543e-18 #> 5   Newton dbldog      1    6    5    5   Fcrit 1.790e-18 #> 6   Newton   hook      1   11    7    7   Fcrit 3.819e-26 #> 7   Newton   none     NA   NA   NA   NA   ERROR        NA #> 8  Broyden  cline      1   17    1   11   Fcrit 2.900e-18 #> 9  Broyden  qline      1   18    1   13   Fcrit 1.404e-17 #> 10 Broyden  gline      1   25    1   11   Fcrit 4.798e-19 #> 11 Broyden pwldog      1   12    1   10   Fcrit 6.230e-18 #> 12 Broyden dbldog      1   12    1   10   Fcrit 3.239e-18 #> 13 Broyden   hook      1   16    1   12   Fcrit 5.719e-23 #> 14 Broyden   none      4   20    1   20 Maxiter 1.846e-01

what: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — zznleqslv-iterationreport

text: format iteration report provided nleqslv trace component control argument set 1.

title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — zznleqslv-iterationreport
text: iteration reports consist series columns header summarising contents. Common column headers list("Iter") Iteration counter list("Jac") Jacobian type. Jacobian type indicated N Newton Jacobian B Broyden updated matrix; optionally followed letter s indicating totally singular matrix letter indicating ill-conditioned matrix. Unless Jacobian singular, Jacobian type followed estimate inverse condition number Jacobian parentheses computed Lapack. column blank backtracking active. list("Fnorm") square euclidean norm function values / 2 list("Largest |f|") infinity norm \(f(x)\) current point

title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — zznleqslv-iterationreport
text: sample iteration report linesearch global methods (cline, qline gline) (intercolumn space removed make table fit) column headed Lambda shows value line search parameter. column headed Ftarg follows sufficient decrease requirement value Fnorm must drop current step accepted. value Lambda may lower threshold determined setting control parameter xtol avoid reporting false convergence. acceptable Lambda possible Broyden method used, new Jacobian computed.

title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — zznleqslv-iterationreport
text: iteration report global method none almost report, except column labelled Ftarg omitted. column Lambda gives ratio maximum stepsize length full Newton step. either exactly 1.0, indicating Newton step smaller maximum stepsize therefore used unmodified, smaller 1.0, indicating Newton step larger maximum stepsize therefore truncated.

what: Report for the double dogleg global method
title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — zznleqslv-iterationreport
text: sample iteration report global method dbldog (intercolumn space removed make table fit) column Jacobian letters indicate following list("C") fraction (\(\le1.0\)) Cauchy steepest descent step taken fraction ratio trust region radius Cauchy steplength. list("W") convex combination Cauchy eta*(Newton step) taken. number column headed Lambda weight partial Newton step. list("P") fraction (\(\ge1.0\)) partial Newton step, equal eta*(Newton step), taken fraction ratio trust region radius partial Newton steplength. list("N") normal full Newton step taken. number column headed Eta calculated upper limit ratio length steepest descent direction length Newton step. See Dennis Schnabel (1996) pp.139ff details. column headed Dlt0 gives trust region size start current iteration. column headed Dltn gives trust region size current step accepted dogleg algorithm. trust region size decreased actual reduction function value norm agree well predicted reduction linear approximation function exhibit sufficient decrease. increased actual predicted reduction sufficiently close. trust region size allowed decrease beyond threshold determined setting control parameter xtol; happens backtracking regarded failure terminated. case new Jacobian calculated Broyden method used. current trust region step continued doubled trust region size actual predicted reduction agree extremely well. indicated * immediately following value column headed Dltn. save gradient calculations. However, trial step never taken current step Newton step. trial step succeed previous trust region size restored halving trial size. exception trial step takes Newton step. case trust region size immediately set size Newton step implies halving new size leads smaller size trust region . Normally initial trust region radius final trust region radius previous iteration trust region size restricted size current Newton step. full Newton steps taken, trust region radius start iteration may less final value previous iteration. double dogleg method trust region updating procedure fully explained sections 6.4.2 6.4.3 Dennis Schnabel (1996).

what: Report for the single (Powell) dogleg global method
title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — zznleqslv-iterationreport
text: sample iteration report global method pwldog (intercolumn space removed make table fit) much simpler double dogleg report, since single dogleg takes either steepest descent step, convex combination steepest descent Newton steps full Newton step. number column Lambda weight Newton step. single dogleg method special case double dogleg method eta equal 1. uses method updating trust region size double dogleg method.

what: Report for the hook step global method
title: Detailed iteration report of nleqslv — zznleqslv-iterationreport
text: sample iteration report global method hook (intercolumn space removed make table fit) column headed mu shows Levenberg-Marquardt parameter Newton step larger trust region radius. column headed dnorm gives Euclidean norm step (adjustment current x) taken algorithm. absolute value difference Dlt0 less 0.1 times trust region radius. column Jacobian letters indicate following list("H") Levenberg-Marquardt restricted step taken. list("N") normal full Newton step taken. meaning columns headed Dlt0 Dltn identical columns double dogleg method. method updating trust region size double dogleg method.
